recordings.subject: S001
recordings.label: Resting — Eyes Open
recordings.task: Subject sitting quietly with eyes open.
recordings.note: Eyes-open rest: alpha is suppressed as the visual cortex processes incoming light. Compare with eyes-closed to see the classic alpha-blocking effect discovered by Hans Berger in 1929.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0

recordings.subject: S001
recordings.label: Resting — Eyes Closed
recordings.task: Subject sitting quietly with eyes closed.
recordings.note: Without visual input the occipital cortex relaxes into a strong rhythmic alpha oscillation (~10 Hz). This idle rhythm is the most robust finding in EEG research.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0

recordings.subject: S003
recordings.label: Motor Imagery — Left Fist
recordings.note: Right motor cortex (C4) desynchronises in the beta band — contralateral to the imagined hand. This ERD is the signal used in BCI prosthetic systems.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0

recordings.subject: S005
recordings.label: Motor Imagery — Right Fist
recordings.note: Left motor cortex (C3) desynchronises. The clear hemispheric asymmetry is what BCI decoders use to classify intended movement direction.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0

recordings.subject: S007
recordings.label: Motor Imagery — Both Fists
recordings.note: Bilateral motor imagery activates both hemispheres symmetrically. Frontocentral electrodes show broad beta suppression.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0

recordings.subject: S009
recordings.label: Motor Imagery — Both Feet
recordings.note: Foot imagery activates medial motor cortex near Cz — the homunculus maps feet to the top of the motor strip, opposite to hand imagery spatially.
recordings.citation: Schalk et al. (2004) BCI2000. PhysioNet eegmmidb/1.0.0
